speaker: interviewer
text: June 4th, 1995. Interview with Olga Kovacs. You were telling us about leaving Budapest and arriving in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: I immediately proceeded to the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is the Hebrew Immigration Association of America. They were very helpful, and we told them we wanted to come to America. And they have known about our relative, because he sent a number of people through their offices, to various parts of this country. And after that, the same day, we went up to the American Embassy. We were smuggling out some old papers, which was also a no-no, because you cannot do that in a Communist-controlled country. But we put it here and there and everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Referring to the old visa registrations which, of course, was silly on our part. Because all they needed is just the telex to Washington, to the State Department, and I'm sure they would have had the numbers. Anyway we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean from 1946 you-- ah, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to have it validated, because they were very low quota numbers, like between 130 and 200-something. So first they said we have to give the passports. We were happy to give over the Hungarian passports, so we became stateless. We got some paper from the-- how did they call it-- the refugee board of the police station in Vienna. And they said they will put things together, and we were notified. It took 9 months to have this done. And in February, they told us that we won't be able to come to America before August. And we were sitting there, since-- we have been sitting there since the previous November. So it was terribly long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought-- it was daring thought, but I went through with it. I wrote a letter to Mrs. John F Kennedy-- Jacqueline Kennedy-- telling her about our problem-- and, as I mentioned, it was in February-- and asking for her help. I'm sure she wasn't in a position to do or to hasten anybody's immigration papers. But I thought I give it a try. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In about two months' time, maybe at the end of April or beginning of May, we got a call from the embassy that we are in the transport, all taken care of, leaving Vienna in three weeks. Whether it was Mrs. Kennedy's office sending our papers to the immigration office, I really don't know. I will never find out, but that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived in America. What day? Do you remember the day that you left Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was either the 22nd and we arrived here on the 23rd, or it was-- I have it in my papers. I believe we arrived here on the 24th--

speaker: interviewer
text: What month was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of May. And it was something. They put us up on a decrepit old military transport plane that kept breaking down. First we had to land in Shannon, because they had some engine trouble. Then we had to land in Gander, Newfoundland because, again, some problems, but we were in America. And it was just-- it's here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Idlewilde. And Mother and Auntie's relative was waiting for us with a furnished apartment, with a fully laden refrigerator. We had a job-- we could have had a job the following day, but the jet lag and the-- I was never so sick in my life than during the journey. We got into one of those famous storms over New York, and we were in holding pattern for two and a half hours. I was so sick, like a dog. Mother and my aunt were holding the fort, but I was a wreck by the time we landed. So within a few days, we got a job. And within five years, we became American citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: First we lived-- I still remember the address. It was 1475 Walton Avenue in The Bronx. And then we found that some very good friends were living in Rego Park in Queens. And there happened to be a vacancy in the same building, so we moved there in '64. And we have lived there-- I lived there with Mother and Auntie 'til October of '75. Then I moved here, and they stayed behind there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in the United States for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother and Auntie started working in a sweat shop. And then gradually they worked up their way to Bloomingdale's, and they were doing finishing work in the alteration department-- patterns and all that. Mother is excellent-- Mother was always excellent at sewing, and my aunt also has been very handy. I started working-- somebody recommended me to work as a store bookkeeper for an export agent-- export-import firm. I hated it. I mean, after the opera. I figured well, Olga, you are in America. You have to start, and then you'll see what happens. So I stayed there until-- from '62 'til '66. By that time, I was familiarized with all the agents, the opera houses, the concert bureaus, and all that. And I had an interview with an agent-- a placement agent-- and he said that I have two jobs for you. One is at the Metropolitan Opera at the financial or administrative department, but not artistic administration-- technical administration. If you want to do it, I can't guarantee you-- and then she repeated-- I cannot guarantee you you would be able to get into artistic administration. However, if you start working at Columbia Artists, that will give you a new perspective. You can build up your contacts. And you'll get an American concert experience. And I chose Columbia. And I was with Columbia from '66-- summer of '66 'til the summer of '72. Then I felt I had to make a change. It was extremely interesting, very educating. And I had some contacts at The Met. But at that time, although they did not tell me you are hired-- it's 100% sure that you will be hired-- it looked as if I would have more chance than not. But then somehow the trail was getting cold. So I figured I'm not going to hang around. I left Columbia. I wrote them a note-- to my boss, who was out of town. I think he was in Europe-- and to the man who was running the personal-- that I'm leaving. I sent my keys in a registered letter. I thanked for everything, and I left on vacation. Right before I left on vacation, I remember I went to Curacao. It was in '66. I told one of my colleagues at Columbia that I will be leaving. And then he said, if I know something, then I'll let you know. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, I come back. I think I went to CBS or ABC because I heard something about their recording department-- CBS Records-- about a job. And I went home and mother told me that, you know who called? Mitch Miller. And-- Was it '66 or '76?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was '66.

speaker: interviewee
text: '66. And I called Mitch, and he said, well, I heard that you left Columbia, and I need a secretary. Would you be interested? So I remember I called him, Maestro. He said, oh, cut that out, Olga. I am not Maestro, I am Mitch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, listen Mitch, I am going on vacation. And then I'll call you when I come back. And I called him back, and that was in '72.

speaker: interviewee
text: From '66 to '72, I was at Columbia. And I have been with Mitch from '72 still. That's the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'd like-- I have a question. What was it like for you adjusting to living in the United States? You spoke English already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke English, but I still have my accent. You can't get rid of the accent, even if you try. The British-American affectation, I can't. I will never be able to pronounce a T-H and all that. But there are certain things that I still don't get, like the schooling system, because I don't have a child. I have never gone through the various intricacies, like the schooling system in America. Otherwise, I love it. I don't agree with most of the things happening today, but I just love it. The first time in our lives, we felt that we were and are welcome in this country. May not agree with Mr. Gingrich and city hall. But it's a beautiful-- it's a marvelous country. It's a good country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt and your mother? They felt the same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they felt they belong here. We were just talking about this with my aunt. I mentioned to you that Auntie is unfortunately in precarious state of health. The other day we were talking about it and she said, oh, you know, I am so happy. And she was the one who really-- I wouldn't say reluctant, but she said, well, I'm coming with you because you are leaving, but she was not enthusiastic. She came along willingly but not enthusiastically. She said, you know, I'm so happy we are here. And I really feel we belong here. And the same for my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was always the strongest among us. She never saw the inside of a hospital, except when they removed my appendix. Mother got some circulatory problems around July in '92. Well, actually the circulatory problems started earlier, but it was under control. But in July of '92, she woke up one day that her left leg is pink-- not regular color, but pink. And it hurt. So she thought it will go away, she strained it, she this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her, Mamica-- I called her Mamica. She called me Mamchika. Mamica, why don't we go to the doc? I meet you there, and we'll take it from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle of July '92. The doctor said she has thrombophlebitis. He called immediately Lenox-- he was associated with Lenox Hill. And mother was admitted the same day. They started treating her leg. And they started administering two kinds of medication. I think one was Coumadin and heparin, some blood thinning medication. And for the rest of my life-- and I shouldn't say that, because I don't have proof-- I will be convinced that mother was mistreated. Because one day I went in, and mother was complaining that her shoulder blade hurt. So I looked at the shoulder blade, and all I saw was just not even a discoloration-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told the doctor. And the doctor said, oh, Mrs. Kovacs, just because you are in bed, after four or five days, you don't have some complaints or some problems because you lie in bed. Mother was moving around. If she had to go to the bathroom, she went to the bathroom. The little that she was allowed to walk, she did. So she wasn't like this in bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the following day, all her side became pitch black. And it was spreading. Her whole side-- the arms, and the leg wasn't getting better either. Somewhat the pinkness receded. But it was still there. But this-- I mean, pitch black. It's not like a bruise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they over-medicated her. And then otherwise, she said that she's feeling OK. That's what she told me. And then she was discharged. And I told her, Mamchika, if you have to go for a check up, why don't you just stay with me? This was her second home. There is not one piece of furniture here that I would have-- except that, because I just got it this year-- that I would have gotten without mother. She was my best friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: She stayed with me here. Within four days, some complications started to come around. And I had to take her by ambulance to the emergency. Of course, they let her wait there forever. And I told them what it is about. Mother should have never been discharged the same day from Lenox Hill. But she was, and there was nothing I could do about it. So we came home. From then on, except one day, which was a Sunday, I remember we were watching some movie on television, and it looked like a normal evening. The blackness started to get red. So I figured maybe, maybe some good will come of this. Mother was 92 on the 2nd of May in '92. And the day after, the complications set in again. And then by the end of the week-- I remember, it was early morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: She loved to sleep here on the sofa. She would never come-- I said, Mamchika, why don't you come? No, no, no. That's your place, and I am better off here. So I went there, and I hugged and kissed her. And I told her, Mamchika, what do you think about-- shouldn't we go to the hospital, to have you treated? All right. So we took a cab, and we went to Lenox Hill and, again, the wait at the emergency. I mean, sick people don't belong to the emergency room. It's horrendous what they do to the people there. It's just simply horrendous. It's everywhere, I understand, not only Lenox Hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then one day-- that was on a Saturday. On Wednesday, I took my vacation. I was with her, starting early, as long as I could, all day.

speaker: interviewer
text: I walk in. And I saw my mother, with the tubes in her nose and the monitoring things and then the heart machine. I said, Mamchika, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: She could hardly talk. She told me, I have terrible chest pain. That's all I needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran around and got the doctor. Mother was in agony, and for 12 minutes, nobody came to help her, absolutely nobody in Lenox Hill. She had a heart attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put her up in intensive care. I went along with her. And they started the various tests. All over her arm, the blood tests and then the tubes, and it was horrendous. And I was hoping for the best. And I spoke to her cardiologist there. The thing is that our cardiologist, who we think the world of, Dr. Jonathan Harper, is at Mt. Sinai. And mother was in no condition to be transported either from here or from Lenox Hill to Mt. Sinai. That's why she wound up at Lenox Hill. Otherwise it would have been Dr. Harper. I spoke to the cardiologist who attended her. And he said it's a serious heart attack, but your mother is a strong lady. She was very frail because of the medication, and because of this, she was always watching. And also I understand with old age, people seem to lose weight. But it was not something pathological about the weight loss.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was on a Wednesday, and I kept going in every day. And on Friday, I had to see my aunt. And my friend Ilsa was staying with Mother in the morning, so I can go to my aunt and do whatever I have to and wanted to do for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the time I got to the hospital on Friday, which was the 21st of August, Mother was rather weak, didn't talk too much. She ate a little, and then she-- and the nurse told me that your mother is making such good progress that from intensive care, she will be put in the-- she told me the night before, on Thursday night, the nurse-- to the regular ward. And, of course, I was more than pleased to hear this. And on Friday, I wanted to stay with her there. And I told her, Mamchika, I'll stay here in the room with you. Or I'll stay outside the-- because they kick you out at 9:00, half past 8:00. No, no, no, my dear child. It will be late, and then I am worried how you get home. And I must have run back to her from the door, I don't know how many times. It was on a Friday. And I was all prepared to go in on Saturday. I was washing my hair, sitting under the dryer, here at the corner of the couch. The phone rang at 10 of 10 in the morning and it was the doctor. I knew immediately it's no good, it's not good. I really must say that I did not expect the terrible news. But I knew that as soon as I heard his name, that it would mean only bad thing. And then he started to say that we did everything we could, and it was your mother's heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I immediately ask him, could I see her? He said, oh yeah, you can see her, meaning-- so I ran there. I couldn't tell you what I felt during the journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remembered that one day I was going to see mother-- that was during her first week of hospitalization-- and I saw a policeman coming towards me in the corridor. And the man next to him, pushing something. And, of course, I'm so nearsighted-- I didn't have these glasses then yet, only my weaker ones-- so as they pulled or walked nearer, then I saw that it was a stretcher and on the stretcher a body bag. It was a horrendous experience, of course, this and visiting my mother. So I went to see my mother. And I must have gotten there just as long as it took me to pull out the rollers and throw something on me and get a cab.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I must have arrived there probably within half an hour. So when I kissed her, her cheeks still blended in with mine. So she was not cold yet. She was still my mother, but she didn't talk to me anymore. And what did you do after she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt was supposed to visit her in the hospital with a friend. And by the time they got there, they found me embracing the dead body of my mother. So we stayed there for a while, and then my aunt stayed with me here. And then we discussed this with Mother earlier. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Mother wanted to be cremated, and her ashes scattered, [SOBBING] just like as it happened to the family in Auschwitz. So we cremated mother. She died on Saturday, on the 22nd. On Sunday, as soon as we came home, I called up Campbell. I didn't know whom to call, the nurse told me in the hospital I should call Campbell. That's why I called-- I didn't know what end was up. I was amazed that I was able to do it and to go through this. And then they said that you have to come here and select the urn and how did you want the arrangements. And that's what happened. We selected an urn, and the cover was a brown-- something like this-- mahogany case. That's what we selected. Nothing elaborate, because that wouldn't have been mother. And we had to identify her. That was horrendous. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we had to wait. And then we got back the ashes, we had to pick up the ashes. And I brought it home. It's right there. I don't have living flowers here. Because of the air conditioning and the blinds and all that. So I pulled out two red roses. I always got her roses for her birthday or for holidays, both her and my aunt. And I put the two roses there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [CRYING] And we sat shiva with my aunt, for Mother. [CRYING] And we lit the candle. I called up-- I called up one of the synagogues, how to go about all this. And that they told us one has to light a candle, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you fulfill her request?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. There is a society in Long Island called the Neptune Society. And they do burial at sea. And a friend of our family drove us out. And with my best friend, Catherine Goldsmith, we met her there. And then the three of us-- the friend couldn't, the friend stayed behind-- and Auntie, Catherine, and I took the boat. And then we went out to sea, a good number of miles from the shore. And I buried mother, in this blue suit with my two hands.
